desc:
Short, green grass generously sprinkled with daisies, chicory and other flowers
stretches out around you. The occasional bushy plant can be seen to the east. To
the west you see that the land is carpeted with pink and white flowers.
name: Short Grass

desc:
Small, bushy plants sprout from the vast plains, their boughs covered with
dangerous looking thistles. The leaves of these plants resemble those of the
familiar holly bush, yet seem to have a slightly lighter hue.
name: Sparse Vegetation

desc:
The grasses which sprout unevenly from the rolling plains are unlike those found
anywhere else in Arda. Small bushes and trees lie scattered amidst this strange,
knee-high ground-covering. The horizon is enshrouded in tangled vegetation and
it is difficult to see beyond a few feet. Towards the east there is an
occasional glimmer, as of light reflecting from water.
name: Sparse Vegetation

desc:
You are on a slight rise in the grass-covered plain, which offers an excellent
view of the surrounding territory. To the northeast lies the crystal blue waters
of the Glanduin, a striking contrast to the endless green ocean of grass which
makes up these plains. An occasional thistle bush makes travelling across this
vast open expanse difficult and painful if the wrong path is chosen.
name: Tall Grassy Plains

desc:
Green and about knee high, the grass here is soft and mixed with various
flowers, including daisies and black-eyed-susans. Butterflies fly back and forth
from flower to flower drinking nectar. Things seem quite peaceful.
name: Short Grass

desc:
This tower appears odd and unconventional from the first glance. It has a
noticeably sloping floor, and the stark, stone blocks that make up the walls
seem to have been defiled by crude drawings, particularly of elves being
tortured and killed. The lack of finesse in these primordial inscriptions seems
to indicate that they were produced by some dark foul beast of Morgoth, who may
have manned this tower long ago. This perch affords an excellent view of the
surrounding country-side.
name: Inside the Watchtower

desc:
Small rocks and pebbles cover this steep hillside, which becomes flatter to the
south. To the northeast a steep cliff seems to sprout from this rise, and to the
south vast, grassy plains extend as far as the eye can see. A strange tower has
been erected to the centre of the plains in the northwest.
name: Steep Hillside

desc:
Verdant, green grass covers this hillside, which offers an excellent view of the
vast plains to the west and the broad river Glanduin to the north, beyond which
lies a steep, rocky slope.
name: Grassy Hillside

desc:
You stand amidst a dense patch of thorny bushes, which tear mercilessly at your
skin. Just to the east lies a grassy hillside, which becomes quite steep to the
northeast, until melding into a sheer cliff face. To the west the air seems to
take on a sickly, brownish hue, accompanied by a disgusting stench.
name: Dense Bushes

desc:
Foul fumes permeate the air around these dark looking waters, which seem to
release a visibly brown gas. The foliage and grass surrounding this area seems
to have taken on this repulsive colour and stench. The occasional bubble of some
strange slimy substance breaks the surface of the pond.
name: Dirty Pond

desc:
Several varieties of grass sprout from the earth here, increasing in height and
colour as the sloping plain approaches a river to the northeast. In all other
directions the rolling grasslands continue as far as the eye can see.
name: Gently Sloping Grass

desc:
The dense earth beneath your feet supports only a short, dense type of grass.
Your view to the north is blocked by a copse of trees, beyond which you can make
out the sound of briskly flowing water. A simple, stone tower rises from the
bleak plains to the northeast.
name: Wide Grassy Plain

desc:
The ground in this natural dip in the plains is very soft and boggy, and only
seems to increase in moisture content as it approaches the river. One of the few
pathways that traverses these wet fields splits here, with spurs going west
south and east. To the west and south the earth seems to dry out slightly.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
Sharp thistles and thorny bushes cover the rocky, dense earth beneath your feet.
To the east lies a steep hillside which leads up to an unscaleable vertical
rise, and to the north lies a swiftly flowing river.
name: Rough Ground

desc:
Tall grasses cover this side of the hill. The peak is just to the south of here,
although it is not a lot higher than this slope. To the west the slope gets
steeper and there seems to be a way down onto the plains. To the east some trees
grow. Just to the north of here there is a cliff edge.
name: Grassy Slope

desc:
A great view of the countryside can be seen in all directions. To the north
there is some kind of steep valley or causeway separating this hillside from the
slightly higher one beyond. To the east there is a woods. To the west a vast
grassy plain stretches as far as the eye can see. The river Glanduin crosses the
plain, presumably it flows along the causeway to the north.
name: Top of the Grassy Hill

desc:
Water from the river Glanduin to the north has flooded into an oval pond cut
into the stone of this hill. Slow eddies drive suds in circular patterns across
this pool's surface. Just below the foam some large fish are swimming about an
old wooden palette searching for a meal. To the north you see some very old
quays. The cliff rises up very steeply to the south preventing movement.
name: Frothing Pool in the Glanduin

desc:
Wispy looking grass sprouts from the ground here, dotted by the occasional
odd-looking bush. Directly to the northeast, a large river emerges from a rocky
valley, bordered by a sheer cliff, and turns to run along the northern edge of
the plain. A small trail seems to follow the river.
name: Sparse Vegetation

desc:
Short, stiff grasses cover this plain that slopes down from a rocky cliff
towards the Glanduin just north of here. There is a road following the opposite
bank of the river, and just beyond that the cliff continues. To the south there
is an impassable gorge. Occasional bushes and shrubs grow among the grasses.
name: Short Grass at the Base of a Cliff

desc:
Tall straight trees grow all about this part of the hillside. The foliage is not
too heavy and grasses grow between the trees. The hillside slopes down to the
northeast where the woods seem to get thicker. To the north there is a gorge,
but thick holly bushes block the way to the northeast along the edge of the
gorge.
name: Wooded Glade

desc:
Tall, leafy trees cover this east facing slope. To the east, at the bottom of
the slope, the trees and undergrowth are extremely dense. Only a very small
animal could penetrate the tangled brush. The top of the slope is bare of trees
but has sparse bits of plains grass growing upon it.
name: Wooded Hillside

desc:
Some animal has excavated a round burrow within the densely packed brush here.
Sharp thorns and a twisty narrow tunnel leading from the brush to the north make
this refuge defendable against most intruders.
name: Dense Burrow in the Brush

desc:
Thick brush grows densely in this secluded valley. The valley walls slope up to
the north and far to the south. A maze of packed brush makes travel difficult to
the south. Holly grows profusely in this valley, especially to the east where
the bushes get thick and almost impenetrable.
name: Thick Brush

desc:
Towering canyon walls block every direction except to the west. The high walls
keep this canyon very dark and some shade can be found at any time during the
day. A few scrub bushes grow in the rocky ground and a small trickle of water
from the top of the canyon suggests at how it was carved. At the base of the
canyon to the west you see a frothy pool.
name: Box Canyon

desc:
Darkness engulfs the entire area beneath the thick bushes. The holly bushes
prevent travel in most directions, but there appears to be a few small animal
tracks through them. Many webs can be seen spanning the branches of some of the
bushes. To the north and the south the land rises a little.
name: Deep inside the Holly Bushes

desc:
Sticky webs hang from branch to branch of the densely packed brush. The bushes
grow so high and thick that no light reaches the ground. There are no obvious
paths to follow that lead out of the undergrowth, apart from the path which
brought you here in the first place.
name: Deep Brush

desc:
To the north the holly bushes end abruptly at the edge of a sheer cliff. This
cliff marks the southern edge of a narrow gorge through which the river Glanduin
flows. To the south the holly undergrowth is thick and hard to penetrate. A
grassy area at the edge of the cliff can be seen just to the east.
name: Thick Brush beside a Cliff

desc:
The holly bushes give way to an open grassy area at the edge of a cliff. There
is a deep gorge just to the northwest which guides the course of the Glanduin.
Far to the northwest a tall hill can be seen, with some strange black object on
top. To the east a maze of twisted holly blocks passage.
name: Opening beside a Cliff

desc:
Bright green holly bushes grow to an incredible height here, blocking out any
light from above. To the north and south the ground rises sharply, but there
does not appear to be any way out of the bushes. To the east and west, the
shallow valley is still choked with holly.
name: Inside the Holly Bushes

desc:
At the top of this small rise, the holly bushes still grow to an amazing height,
blocking out nearly all the sky. To the north the ground appear to dip down into
a densely vegetated valley running from east to west. East of here the holly
seems to become slightly less dense, while to the west a dark clump of bushes
seems to effectively block travel in this direction.
name: A Dense Patch of Holly

desc:
Dense holly bushes grow on this north facing slope of a secured valley. Although
they grow quite high, it is possible to see the sky occasionally. However, at
the few places that offer any kind of view across the valley, the only thing
that can be seen is holly.
name: A Dense Patch of Holly

desc:
Growing straight and tall, the trees here block all light from the sky above.
Thick bushes make any kind of movement both painful and difficult. The
occasional movement of a bush or chirp of a bird reminds you of the many small
animals that inhabit this forest.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
Tall trees grow profusely in this dark forest, blocking out all sight of the sky
above. Bushes grow thickly at ground level, making travel in any direction
difficult. There are, however, occasional animal pathways which aid movement
through the bushes.
name: Towering Forest

desc:
Thickly packed trees and overgrown bushes make passage very difficult through
this part of the forest. The dense foliage overhead blocks out all sign of the
sky, making the forest very dark and gloomy.
name: Towering Forest

desc:
The thick holly bushes grow very high in this part of the valley, blocking out
the sky completely. They also block travel in most directions quite well, some
small animal paths seem to lead off to the west and south. To the east a thick
tangle of holly bushes clusters around the stump of an old tree.
name: In the Holly Bushes

desc:
A foul stench permeates the air all around here. It smells as if some kind of
swamp is nearby, but the ground feels firm enough here. Dense foliage covers the
sky, making the forest very dark. Unusually, fewer bushes seem to be growing in
this part of the forest, although a thick tangle of holly bushes is concentrated
on the western side of the forest.
name: Dark Towering Forest

desc:
Twisting about each other, the trees block many routes through the forest.
Progress is slow, and backtracking is often required to find a path big enough
to allow passage. The bushes growing all over the area hamper movement even
further.
name: Twisty Forest

desc:
Dense holly bushes block most of the pathways through the forest. However,
progress can be made, albeit very slowly. The branches of the trees far above
entwine in an intricate pattern, blocking nearly all the light from above, so it
is difficult to tell if it is night or day above the treetops.
name: Edge of the Holly Forest

desc:
This path has been cleverly hidden amidst the tangles of holly and thistles
which grow densely packed all around. Though this path seems seldom trodden, it
is kept in fair condition. To the north the path ascends up the gentle rise of
the cliff's southern flank.
name: Secret Path up the Cliff

desc:
Damp rotting logs line the edge of this sheltered glade in the dark forest. Tall
trees block the view in all directions, but the ground seems to slope up to the
northeast.
name: Grassy Clearing in the Forest

desc:
The forest here is dark and gloomy. The trees grow tall here, blocking out any
light from above. Lots of dense foliage makes travel awkward, although the
numerous animal paths that criss-cross the area help a little.
name: Forested Hillside

desc:
Grey rocky slopes surround the faint animal trails that cross the area. The
slopes to the south and the west are very steep, and climbing them would be
dangerous. However, shallower slopes allow passage to the east and the north. In
the far southwest some grassy plains cover the area down to a major river.
name: Rocky Hillside

desc:
Small holly bushes cover this grassy clearing. It is, however, quite easy to
avoid them whilst crossing the grass. To the south, east and west the bushes get
thicker, possibly even impassable. The river Glanduin can be seen clearly a few
leagues to the north.
name: Holly Bushes

desc:
Holly bushes grow in abundance here and their red berries stand out on the
backdrop of the green leaves. The bushes grow most densely to the east and south
and prevent any form of passage in those directions. Far to the west a dense
forest can be made out.
name: Holly Bushes

desc:
Rugged grasses cover this slight slope. Many holly bushes dot the area, some
have grown quite large on the windy plain. To the north, grasslands continue to
dominate the scene, bordered on the east by the Glanduin. To the west, a dense
forest can be seen.
name: Grassy Scrubland

desc:
Holly bushes can be seen growing all over this low lying area just south of the
Glanduin. Between the bushes, small paths can be made out, allowing comfortable
travel through this area. All these paths seem to follow an east- west route.
Upon close examination, these paths appear to have been made by some humanoid
being.
name: Grassy Scrubland

desc:
A well used muddy path extends from the shallow part of the Glanduin in the
north and turns westwards. As the trail leaves the muddy shores of the river,
the trail peters out and becomes increasingly difficult to follow. Grasslands
and holly bushes stretch out to the east, making up the southern banks of the
river.
name: Path through the Scrubland

desc:
Sparse holly bushes grow all over this plain beside the River Glanduin. Beyond
the river the ground rises upwards, and from the straight lines that crisscross
the surface, it looks as if it has been segregated into fields.
name: Grassy Scrubland

desc:
Prickly-leafed holly bushes wreath this small clearing, hugging it on all sides
and hiding it from view. Beyond the bushes to the south, some tall, slender
trees rise up, fighting for position in the sunlight. The sky is directly
overhead, unhidden by branches or leaves. The grass here is soft and downy, and
the earth gives just enough to be comfortable.
name: Clearing amongst Holly Bushes

desc:
The holly bushes make passage difficult here, their thorns hindering any
movement. Small animal trails criss-cross the undergrowth and travel through
small natural tunnels formed by the holly bushes. The holly bushes continue to
the north, but peter out to the east and give way to grasslands. The density of
the bushes to the west denies any passage in that direction.
name: Holly Bushes

desc:
This small building appears to be some sort of small temple for the inhabitants
of the village. A few rough benches make the pews and a table at the back of
building forms a pulpit. The building appears to be in a sorry state of repairs,
with holes in the walls and roof which let in the rain.
name: Wooden Temple

desc:
Here lies a village, hidden from outside view by the surrounding bushes and
trees. Apparently, someone doesn't want to be found. To the north, a small
wooden hut has been built and the village continues to the west.
name: Secluded Village

desc:
A wide open field seems to be the site of many recent skirmishes, although it
isn't obvious what creatures the skirmishes are between. However, a closer
inspection of some of the constructions that litter the area suggests that the
field is used to practice battle. A small wooden hut lies to the north, a thin
column of smoke rising from a small chimney on its roof.
name: Village Training Ground

desc:
This hut appears sturdy, but the inside is messy. Dirty cutlery and clothes lie
haphazardly about the room suggesting that cleanliness is not a high priority
for the inhabitants of this building. Several rough beds lie along one side of
the hut.
name: Wooden Hut

desc:
The underground stream ends here and flows out the northern wall through a small
hole near the floor. Only very small animals could travel through the small
hole, leaving only two obvious ways out, either the cave to the west or the
ladder leading up to a trapdoor.
name: Underground Cavern

desc:
This area seems to have been dug out of the softer earth here, but bare stone
can be seen forming the walls and roof here, so no supports are required. This
area seems to have little traffic, as there are few tracks here on the dirt
floor. Those tracks that are here lead towards a rough door in the south wall.
name: Secret Room

desc:
This apparently makes up a store room. Shelves line the walls and hold various
mundane items required for a small village like salt, food and utensils. The
room continues to the west.
name: Store Room

desc:
The store room continues on here but ends at a stone wall to the west. More
shelves line the walls filled with yet more supplies for the village. You can
leave this part of the store to the east.
name: Back of the Store Room

desc:
This room is apparently an armoury for someone. Various racks on the walls seem
made to hold various weapons, mostly shortswords, bows and daggers. Small pieces
of broken weapons litter the floor.
name: The Armoury

desc:
The cavern makes a sharp turn here, leading from the south to the east. The
water travels slowly round the bend, creating small whirlpools as it goes.
name: Bend in the Underground Stream

desc:
The water continues its underground passage here, leading northwards. The water
travels along a wider part of the floor here and so runs a little slower. The
cavern walls are smooth here, apparently hewn out of the rock by centuries of
water flow.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
The water makes a turn here, travelling from west to north down a gradual slope
in the cave. The water flow is fast but kept to a narrow ditch in the centre of
the cavern, a ditch which can easily be crossed by even the shortest legs.
name: Bend in the Underground Stream

desc:
Water pours out of a hole in the western end of this cave. The water has none of
the power that must have carved this cavern out of the solid rock from which it
is made. The water travels down a small channel to the east.
name: Source of the Stream

desc:
The fall of the northern wall has left what few pieces of this rampart that
remain in a tenuous position. There is nothing other than the rubble of the wall
to support this elevated ledge; it is apparent that a single misstep could bring
what is here crashing down. At one time this rampart continued on to the east.
That portion, however, appears to be completely destroyed. The only advantage to
this position is its prime placement for viewing the Hollin Road which lies
below, yet unreachable, to the north.
name: Rampart's End

desc:
With the wall below destroyed, this narrow rampart is more of a badly decayed
bridge than a sturdy passageway of this ancient city. The floor is cracked and
in some places there are holes wide enough for a careless traveller to fall
through. Loose stones from parts of the wall both above and below lay, untouched
for centuries, covered in moss. Vines that start below have scaled their way up
through the floor and attached themselves to both stone and other vines.
name: Narrow Remains of a Rampart

desc:
The rampart is rather sturdy here. It is snuggled into the wall corners and
seems to have survived the brute force of whatever attack demolished the city.
However, age has significantly worn the stones and moss grows heavily here along
with vines.
name: Northwest Corner of a Broken Rampart

desc:
This vaulted tower once held an enormous silver bell. A few strands of the ropes
used to ring the bell still dangle overhead from the only remaining rafter. It
runs from the northeast corner of where the roof once began to the southern edge
of the eastern wall. Only the eastern wall is entirely intact. The northern and
western walls are only half as high as they once were and the exposed edges
continue to crumble. The southern wall is gone entirely giving an unobstructed
view of the now overgrown city.
name: Vaulted Belfry

desc:
Twisted metal rods that are bent and battered lie across the path. They appear
to be part of a railing which cautioned travellers that down from this scenic
walk way was the southern cliff and the rivers that lay beneath it. Now they
serve little use. In fact, they only hinder passage on this overgrown path cut
deep into the river bluff.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
Crumbling and overrun by bushes and shrubs, which have grown taller than some
young trees, is the front facade of a single structure. Openings in this brick
walling, most likely doors, indicate this building may have housed several
families. What is left of the foundation slopes southward. The southern end of
the slab is far more decayed that the northern. Below here is a meandering
footpath and to the east a long abandoned walkway.
name: Hillside Berth

desc:
The two walls of a single structure is all that is left standing of what appears
to have been a small development of homes. Less than six foundations in all,
these homes seem to have been larger than those to the west judging by the
distance between piles of stone rubble. Grasses that may once have been well
kept lawns grow wild around and on top of the remains of this civilisation.
name: Fallen Hillside Hamlet

desc:
Some venerable trees grow here in rows, equidistant from one another as they
descend the hillside toward the south. Mounds of bricks, in piles numerous
enough to each represent the fall of a small home, accompany each tree. The
trees that look to have been planted by the homeowners now overwhelmingly
surpass the ruinous foundations in both height and longevity.
name: Hillside Stand of Trees

desc:
This granite slab, more than a foot thick, is all that remains of this
structure. The shape of the foundation shows it to be divided into four
sections, most likely rooms. At the edge of one such section some writings are
carved into the slab; the rest of the foundation is smooth. The walls appear to
have been sliced off, in contrast to the structures to the west which have
crumbed overtime. So little debris from the structure is left it seems as if
this place was swept clean before being left to be overrun by vegetation. One
can only imagine what type of weaponry could strip this structure away so
completely. An overwhelming sweet smell emanates from several of the nearby
flowering plants.
name: Vestige of a Grand Estate

desc:
A stone hearth, and part of the wall that surrounds it, stands here almost
completely obscured by large bushes and shrubs. Once part of a quaint elven home
it now sits upon a shattered foundation. The roots of a large tree, that began
as a tiny sprout growing between the cracks, have over time broken apart more of
this home's foundation.
name: A Scorched Hearth

desc:
This is the foundation of a small structure, only the arched framework of the
entrance remains. Two modest cylindrical columns hold up a single rectangular
granite block. A creeping ivy has grown up one of the columns, spread across and
down the other. The entryway to this place is of very little consequence
however, considering the walls of this structure are long gone; not a single
granite brick remains. Amidst the plants that cover much of the foundation are a
few shards of metal. Upon closer examination they appear to be bits and pieces
of a silver sword. The blade is splintered beyond repair and only the hilt
remains.
name: Ransacked Estate

desc:
This portion of the narrow footpath leads north to a large granite slab, from
its size most likely the foundation of a small structure. In the shadow of the
hillside above, the path looks dark as night with the exception of flakes of
pulverised granite. Most likely the flakes are what is left of the granite
bricks that would have formed the walls of the structure. Even through the
sporadic grass, the way the granite flakes glisten in the moonlight preserves a
certain amount of majestic nobility that was long ago vanquished. The path
continues on to the east.
name: Speckled Path

desc:
Running east to west this narrow avenue seems to be a means of passage through
what appears to have been the garden district of this city. The path bends just
briefly to the north before a large granite slab. Flakes of what looks to be the
same type of granite are mixed into the dirt of this path giving it a marbleised
or speckled appearance.
name: Speckled Path

desc:
Mixed in with the dirt and under the scramble of wild weeds tiny specks of
granite chips can be seen embedded in a long ago well-travelled path. The flakes
cover much of the ground even outside the bounds of the path but are more
heavily concentrated to the north near the remains of a small home. The flakes
seem to have been pieces of the granite bricks that once made up this humble
abode.
name: Speckled Path

desc:
This narrow walkway carved into the side of the bluff seems to have been built
on an angle just right for viewing the River Estelin to the south. The rushing
waters sometimes weave a cool mist that in summer cool the air along the
walkway. A few bars of iron are all that is left of what appears to be a guard
rail that stopped onlookers from getting too close to the dangerous cliff.
Centuries ago this would have been an easy stroll. Tough weeds have grown into
large bushes that obstruct much of the path which leads westward.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
What twisted metal is left of a once dark wrought iron railing warns travellers
of the steep drop to the south. Below in that direction lies the River Sirannon.
The railing follows the length of this street until it ends most abruptly, like
the land to the north, by falling piece by piece, and in this case cobblestone
by cobblestone, into the river below. In places where the stones are all gone
and the underlying dirt rinsed away by wind and rain one can see the cracks in
the granite plate. The cracks seem to grow wider further to the north.
name: Where the Sidewalk Ends

desc:
Just like the land to the north, here too the remains of the city and the land
on which it stood are falling over time into the River Sirannon which lies below
to the east. Bushes and trees alike make futile attempts to dig their roots into
the decaying soil of the bluff. Many trees have already met their demise falling
into the fast flowing river along with crumbling blocks of granite that lay
scattered in piles of four and five. Moss and fungus that thrive in such wet
conditions have made the ground nearest the edge extremely slippery. Without
warning the soil could shift under the feet of those peering over to catch a
glimpse of the water below.
name: Eroding Bluff

desc:
Nothing is left standing at this point on the bluff, not the city wall nor the
trees that grew years later in its place. Now even the small bushes of holly are
falling into the river below to the east. All that survives is a thick covering
of moss. At one time this area must have been more stable. Where all the dirt
has washed away one can see deep cracks in the granite plate that lies under
this entire area. Clearly this was not caused by a force of nature as the crack
begins several yards in from the edge and spreads outward rather than cracking
at the edge of the bluff where the land would have been naturally weaker. One
could assume such a force intended the cracks to splinter further pulling
everything that stood here for miles around slowly into the river.
name: At the Source of the Crack

desc:
The gargantuan vines that cover this area, making it almost impassable, seem to
emanate from the west. Their widths range in diameter from the size of a wizards
staff to that of the trunk of an ancient tree. They cling to dead trees no doubt
strangled by the weight of these enormous tentacles. Using the trees as leverage
they stretch up from the ground some thirty feet into the air. Some of the lower
vines are marred with scratches that appear to have been made by a swift animal
as it managed to negotiate the spaces between them. The scratches seem to have
had a negligible effect on the rampant vines. The cuts are covered with a clear
liquid, most likely sap from the healthy vines, creating a natural bandage over
the exposed portions.
name: Entangled Ruins

desc:
A few pieces of glass that look to be many centuries old lie on the ground
outside a mostly obscured iron framework. The thick metal rods are twisted
horribly out of shape but the frame is intact. The cause of this distortion is
easily apparent; vines with some stems wider then the base of a well have
sprouted out from inside what appears to have been a structure made completely
of iron and glass. From the arrangement of the ruins and the variety of plant
life, this must have been a place of cultivation, an arboretum of sorts. At one
time it can be assumed these vines, along with other choice vegetation, were
kept tame, pruned by their caretaker. Since their abandonment they must have
first grown to the size of the structure and then over the ages as they gathered
strength pushed outward expanding their habitat a great distance.
name: Entangled Framework

desc:
This area is entirely engulfed by a large system of vines. They grow thicker in
the east and thin out slightly, in number and size, as they reach westward. So
consuming are these monstrous vines that they have choked the life out of the
trees; their heavy tentacles cover the branches in a serpentine manner, crushing
any budding leaves before they can unfold. Nothing seems a hindrance to their
rampant growth, not even the deep cuts in the portions that lay low to the
ground. Most likely caused by the claws of a small swift animal able to sneak
between the strands, sap quickly covers the cuts sealing the exposed area like a
bandage.
name: Entangled Ruins

desc:
A wall, created by some enormous vines woven around and in between several dead
trees, makes passage northward extremely difficult. Clearly the vines were the
cause of the trees demise. So tightly wound around them are the tendrils of
these vines that any new buds of life were quickly strangled leaving the tree
with no leaves to gather what the trees needed from the sun. It is quite
possible, as the vines continue to grow upward, that at some future point they
will pull what remains of these trees to the ground. A few granite bricks can be
seen buried deep under the vines covering the ground. Watchers with a quick eye
might see the head or tail of some swift animals small enough to dart through
these vines without becoming entangled. Their sharp claw marks are apparent on
some of the larger vines.
name: Entangled Ruins

desc:
It must have taken the weaponry of a great army to smash through this great city
wall with such force as to push the bricks several yards across the city
grounds. Not only did the wall separate at the spaces between the bricks but
also shattered individual bricks at the point of impact. Much of the plant life
that was once kept out of the city by the wall has breached the gap and now this
place is more a continuance of the forest region than a city suburb. Creeping
vines from the south have crawled over the widespread rubble.
name: Breach in the Crumbling Wall

desc:
The sounds of the fast flowing River Sirannon seem to call the dirt from this
bluff down into its depths. What destabilised this land is unknown, but it
appears to have happened long ago, perhaps during the great war of Sauron and
the Elves. Trees along the eastern edge, that have not yet fallen into the
water, lean this same direction. Their roots desperately cling to the remaining
soil. Moss and other fungi have flourished in this moist environment. A few
scattered granite blocks remind visitors that there was once a great wall around
this place. The crumbled remains of the wall along with what seems to be another
structure blocks passage northward.
name: Mossy Granite Bluff

desc:
Large thick bushes dominate this small square. In the centre is a large granite
block which looks to have been the pedestal of a statue; only the feet of the
figure remain. The knee-high base seems to have some writing etched into it.
Outward from the statue, under the weeds and grass, are granite stepping stone
paths leading outward in all four directions. Tall trees sporadically line the
paths - some appear to be as old as Arda itself judging by their trunk size.
name: A Place of Remembrance

desc:
Eight granite columns, as tall as some of the younger trees in this area, form a
circle around this place. Several are chipped in many places but none of the
pillars are broken or missing. Inside the circle formed by these towering
granite spires are large stone steps descending down many feet to a central flat
circular stone. The cascade of steps must have at one time surrounded the centre
stone in all directions. Now, the eastern side is little more than a mix of
dirt, weeds, and crumbled stone. On the northern side an entire section has been
destroyed by a large fallen tree; it shattered each of the stone steps it landed
upon from the highest down to just above the lowest. The southwestern part of
this amphitheatre remains for the most part intact. A traveller with a passion
for ancient history might consider sitting here upon the steps and imagining the
great performances that once took place.
name: Pillared Enclave

desc:
The variety and sheer number of plants, even in their unkempt and vastly
overgrown condition, gives evidence that this may have once been a park. The
roots of each seem equidistant from each other, contributing to the notion that
they were planted and left to run rampant. Large horizontal stone blocks, both
shattered and crumbling, frame this gathering area. The faint sound of rushing
water can be heard emanating from the south.
name: Beside an Old Stone Bench

desc:
A perfect vantage point from which to see down the sloping hillside to the
south, the citizens of this city may have sat here watching the boats that at
one time sailed along the river. Now, all that can be seen is the crumbling
foundations where homes must have once stood and the overgrown footpaths not
travelled for centuries. Some crystalline lattice work appears on the ground, a
mix of glass and some unknown element. From the criss-cross design and the
pattern of its destruction it is conceivable this was a large gazebo. A powerful
sweet smell emanates from a large grouping of flowering plants that may have
once been planted around this area. These now grow wild and cover much of the
ground here.
name: Splintered Trellis Gazebo

desc:
A vast number of berry bushes cover this parcel of land. They seem to have grown
unrestrained for centuries. Most are more than a story tall. The bushes mingled
with wild grasses and vines form a small jungle nestled here within the ruins of
the old city. A few bits of broken stone lie under all this greenery, hidden
waiting to trip up weary travellers.
name: Bygone Parkland

desc:
Enormous pine, fir, and cedar trees arch in lush profusion over a scarcely
recognisable trail winding in an east-west fashion from courtyard to gate. All
save a few towering trees are draped in moss. Wildflowers and foliage blanket
the ground in all directions. Just off the trail lies a slender stone beam
broken in several places, perhaps the remains of a park bench.
name: Arched Timbers

desc:
Flowering plants that cover the entire spectrum of colours fill this forgotten
place. The number of different varieties leads one to believe many of these
plants are not native to this area. Probably imported for their beauty, left to
run rampant some of the heartier bushes have clearly dominated this park setting
making this area a small jungle of sorts. Through the jumble of greenery one can
see the remains of a structure to the northwest. The grounds in that direction
look more manageable.
name: Forgotten Flora

desc:
Pieces of what seem to be an old stone marker are strewn among a variety of
bushes and wild flowering weeds. Other slabs of stone, now crumbled to what
appears to be a quarter of their original size, are arranged in two rows on
either side of the markers' resting place. Moss covers much of the exposed stone
and several stones are worn down to just a few inches above the ground. Perhaps
these were benches upon which the residents of this city sat to reflect upon
their days. Being located in a central spot of the eastern portion of the city
this area gets a great deal of light except in the afternoons when, as the sun
descends, it is blocked by the remains of a great tower to the west.
name: Sky Lit Courtyard

desc:
Nestled under what looks to have been a rampart attached to the city wall all
four walls of this building remain standing, although the roof is gone and the
walls do not stand to their full height. The tops of each are crumbling from
exposure to the elements not the ravages of war that destroyed the rampart
above. The stone slabs here seem to been head and foot boards of beds stacked
one upon the other. They were four beds high and there are almost twenty rows.
None of the bedding remains and one might assume the slats on which the bedding
had lain were made of a material that decayed over the ages. Judging from the
narrow width and the elongated space from head to foot these clearly were the
very modest resting places of the city guards.
name: Sheltered Barracks

desc:
Large bricks, that once formed the walls of this tower, litter the ground. Only
the north wall remains intact though badly eroded by the elements. Rubble piled
almost as high as the walls that originally formed this tower are all that
remains of the toppled walls to the south and east. Though badly damaged a stone
stairway leading to the top of the tower remains; perhaps the fact that it was
attached to the one surviving wall helped to preserve it. A large silver bell is
on the floor, one side completely shattered. It looks to have fallen the great
distance from the belfry above.
name: Foundation of the Bell Tower

desc:
Mangled wrought iron, as black as the night sky, is all that is left of the
fence that once stood between the cobblestone street to the west and what
appears to have been a great eastern lawn dividing the city's two halves. It
stood as a marker between the part of the city used as commerce and that part in
which the citizens spent their leisure time. Now mostly obscured by vegetation
it still serves as a means of division. The broken fence has an abundance of
sharp edges that seem to catch anything passing by on the wind. Small shreds of
clothing that appear to have been torn from the attire of present day travellers
caution passage here might be painful.
name: A Tangle of Iron

desc:
A dotting of trees and weeds growing waist high obscure the sharp spokes of
wrought iron. This was once part of a great fence that divided the old city in
two halves. Even against the dark colour of the metal shards one can see the
stains of dried blood, pieces of cloth ripped from the clothes of latter day
travellers, and animal fur. Passage over this tangle of iron and greenery might
prove painful even to the most careful of beings. To the east, one can see
nothing but wild weeds and moss covered trees and stone. The fencing seems to
have ended here to the south. A pile of what appears to be wood lies here under
a mass of mildew and fungus, home to a variety of mushrooms.
name: A Trapping of Iron

desc:
The cobblestone street to the north gives way to this winding walkway leading
down and along the river bluff. Several stones from the street, over time, have
found their way here to rest alongside the wild weeds and grasses. Whether this
path ever contained similar stones is unknown. It is more likely that it was
dirt or perhaps even gravel. It seems to have been designed more for aesthetics
then usefulness. To the west or following the river walk downward one might get
a better look at the confluence of rivers that border this high place.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
Worn smooth with age and overgrown to the point of decay, this granite walkway
travels up and down along the western rim of a large hillside basin. A mangled
wrought iron fence, bent and twisted to the point of barbs, still prevents the
careless passer-by from falling over the edge into the fast flowing river below.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
With the structures intact, this footpath was too small to traverse any way but
single file. Whether it was planned or just good fortune, this served as an
advantage slowing the attack of the encroaching army, perhaps long enough for
some of the residents to escape. Overgrown with weeds, the path's dirt
foundation contains a sprinkling of granite chips that might once have been part
of the blocks that formed the hillside estates. It is quite likely that the
forces that lay siege to this city for several years tired of negotiating this
passageway and in turn decided to demolish the structures making passage easier.
This would explain the fact that not even a single complete brick of granite can
be found.
name: Speckled Path

desc:
This entire surrounding area resembles a shallow bowl. This parcel in the centre
portion is rather flat. The base of what may have been an old fountain lies
amidst a mass of flowering bushes. Trees ranging in age from before the cities
founding to sapling just sprouting dot the landscape. A small area is sectioned
off by a wrought iron gate. The gate now traps some small weeds grown into
shrubs, however at one time it may have provided a safe play area for children
keeping them far away from the rushing river not too far to the south.
name: Abandoned Playground

desc:
The granite flakes that create this paths speckled appearance lessen toward the
south as it meets a weaving walk along the river bluff. Left abandoned and
rarely travelled, trees have sprouted up where the residents once strolled. Wild
weeds grow unrestrained yet this path survives even under the vegetation.
name: Speckled Path

desc:
Carved into the river bluff this sloping portion of the path does not offer
quite as grand a view of the rivers as further down the walk to the south. In
the mornings the abundance of water helps to weave a mist obscuring the wrought
iron railing designed to keep travellers from getting too close to the edge.
While the rail is broken and many sections are missing is still serves a
cautionary purpose.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
An entire section of stones is gone from this cobblestone street. The missing
stones lie in a dishevelled pile along the eastern edge. This section of what
appears to have been the city's main thoroughfare is one of the most badly
damaged. Although most of the cobblestones remain in their places, they lie
there shattered. The gaps left between them have allowed numerous sprouts of
grasses and large weeds. The street carries on to the west to what looks to have
been the centre of commerce for this city.
name: Southeast Corner of the Cobblestone Street

desc:
This fragile precipice is only half intact. The eastern side has long ago fallen
into the river below. A broken railing, made of black wrought iron, dangles off
the edge reaching down several feet. Gazing over the edge to the west one can
see water gushing from a chute in the bluff. The water must pass under this spot
before cascading out in a great waterfall. The ground here vibrates from the
pressure of the water's flow.
name: Waning Overlook

desc:
Many of the cobblestones in this portion of the street are loose, allowing grass
and extraneous flowering weeds to sprout between them. Moss grows rampant as
well, flourishing in the moisture that has now been allowed to escape from below
the once tightly aligned stones. In some places one must look straight down to
even see the cobblestone street hidden under the greenery. In other spots there
are piles of stones that have been pushed aside by the weeds, which have over
time grown into large shrubs. The stones serve as a marker of the care that must
have been taken to lay this mosaic-like street. Toward the south is an overlook
that serves as a vantage point from which one can view the rushing water that
can be heard from here.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
It is clear to see by the arrangement of the dirt coloured stones that whomever
made up this street was a meticulous person. Even after thousands of years one
can still see that the natural stones were carefully sorted by size before being
placed in almost perfect rows. Many of the stones are chipped by what could have
been the careless marching of an army through this street. Whatever chipped
these stones no one remained behind to repair or replace them.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
Only a very small portion of these chipped stairs are climbable. Most are so
badly crumbled that any weight would cause them to practically disintegrate.
Looking carefully a route can be plotted, skipping a step here and there, to
take someone from this point at the base to the level above. Trying to picture
their original state, one can see that these steps served a decorative purpose.
The elongated widths and gentle slope create a notion of grandeur rather than
expedience of use or simple functionality. Ending at the first step is the
cobblestone street that encircles what is left of this part of the city. Looking
down one might be mesmerised by the lack of change in the pattern of stones
making up this street. While it makes some rather sharp turns continuing on in
three directions, it is difficult to discern where the stones turn so that the
rows of stones on each street all go the same direction.
name: Remains of the City Steps

desc:
This piece of the cobblestone street must have seen a lot of traffic before this
city's tragic end. Along the passageway between the city gate and the steps
leading to the cultural centre of the city the stones bear the marks of horses
hooves as well as age. Several stones are missing, perhaps shattered and their
remains blown away. In the holes that remain wild weeds and grasses have shot up
in their place.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
There is a wrought iron railing that closely follows along the southern section
of the carefully laid cobblestone street. Twisted and broken in places, it still
serves its purpose of warning travellers that a river lies several feet below.
The sound of rushing water breaks the monotonous silence of this now dead city.
Stepping close to the rail one can see a rising mist and feel the occasional
spray of water. To the north appears to be an entrance to the lower portion of
the ruined city.
name: Cobblestone Street beside an Iron Railing

desc:
Totally encompassed in mould and mildew, and left to fester over centuries, this
entire room gives off a putrid smell. Even the fiercest of creatures would be
humbled by this abominable muck. A long stone countertop that may have once lain
atop a wooden counter now lies in pieces on the floor. One can only guess this
may have been a grocery of some sort. The door to the south opens up on to the
city's cobblestone street. A stairway leading up may have been a means of
supplying produce to the restaurant above.
name: Grotesque Remains of a Grocery

desc:
The cobblestone street carries on both to the east and west. This section has
withstood the centuries well; only a few stones are chipped. In one place
several granite blocks, apparently once part of the structure to the north, have
fallen with such great force through the cobblestones that they have imbedded
themselves deeply into the granite plate below. There is the sound of rushing
water from the confluence of the rivers Estulin and Sir Lontalaith, it breaks
the deafening silence that echoes through this city which was long ago
vanquished.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
The city street extends like a shelf over the edge of the bluff. Looking over
the mangled wrought iron rail to the east one can see a thick spray of water
gushing from a chute some thirty feet below. So fast does the water flow out
from the bluff that its spray can be felt even here. It crashes into the water
below creating a rising mass of foam. Below and to the south and west are
equally fast flowing rivers.
name: Misty Embrasure

desc:
This quiet corner of cobblestone street is bordered on the west side by what is
left of the old city wall. It stretches at least two stories high and protects
this area from the forest on the other side. The branches of some very tall
trees can be seen peering their way over the crumbling wall. In several places
lower tree limbs have pushed through the wall knocking out blocks that were
already weak with age. To the south is a mangled portion of wrought iron
railing. The railing is too weak to hold anyone back from falling into the water
below, but still serves as a warning sign of the steep drop.
name: Southwest Corner of the Cobblestone Street

desc:
In the morning the light of the sun against what is left of the tower above to
the east looks as if it would cast a dark shadow over this piece of street. The
city wall to the west is completely destroyed. The blocks of granite have
crumbled one upon another the weight of each serving to further crush one
another. Once two stories high, in its current ruinous state it still serves to
protect the city from the outside world. Only a few spaces just large enough for
small animals to negotiate break the isolation.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
Weeds have forced their way past the stones that form this street. Long
abandoned, no one remained to pull the green sprouts as they first emerged. The
tight arrangement of the stones by the street's builder, however, has kept the
weeds small. Rather than overtaking the street they are limited to the spaces
between the stones that have formed throughout years of neglect.
name: Cobblestone Street along the Wall

desc:
There is a large piece of soot covered granite wall that protrudes through the
ceiling of this room. Narrow stone shelves that once lined the walls now lay
broken on the ground, knocked lose by a sharp impact. The entire western wall
has been pushed into this room leaving the exit west completely exposed. Dirt
from the street has collected against the eastern wall. Some worn stone steps
lean upward.
name: Regressed Emporium

desc:
A piece of the wall that surrounded this city stands three stories above the old
cobblestone street. In most places the wall is just rubble, though still high
enough to protect what is left of this place. Here one can see the granite
bricks arranged one on top of the other in a clover design with one central
brick and four of a similar shade of granite on each of its sides.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
This is the northwest corner of the once great city of Ost-in-Edhil. The tall
city wall that ran along the north and down the western boundary has fallen
inward here blocking half the street. The piles of granite blocks though no
longer neatly arranged one on top of the other still serve to protect the city
from the surrounding forest land. A small portion of wall still standing shows
the meticulous care its builders took in the perfect arrangement of light and
dark stones. Where the granite blocks have fallen the cobblestones are
completely shattered.
name: Northwest Corner of the Cobblestone Street

desc:
The cobblestones that make up this street are each about the length and width of
an elven hand. They must have been carefully laid stone by stone over the course
of many months. The dirt coloured stones lie one hundred across, setting the
width of the ancient street. So close are they placed together that even after
all these centuries very few sprouts of grass have pushed there way between the
stones. To the south beside the remains of one of the city structures is a stone
cart. Too heavy itself to have been used for carrying things from place to place
it was most likely just for show.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
Rubble from both the old city wall and large pieces of granite which have fallen
from the structures above combine and almost completely block this portion of
street. Passage is possible only by walking close to the south along the ruins
of an old shop as the rubble concentrates to the northern portion of the street.
Though the arched doorway is partly obstructed, entrance to the shop looks
possible. Hinges attached to the stone give evidence of perhaps a wooden door
that has long ago decayed into nothingness. Above the door etched into the stone
is some writing.
name: Cobblestone Street amid the Rubble

desc:
A mess of pulverised stone along with battered and broken pieces of metal block
off half this room. Behind the rubble, out of reach, is a large blackened anvil
accompanied by the heads of several hammers. The wooden handles must have
decayed long ago. Large brass hooks embedded into the crumbling walls look
strong enough to have held shields and pieces of armour. What is here now, for
the most part, is completely useless.
name: Remiss Armoury

desc:
Several rows of cobblestones have fallen down into what seems to be a large
crater. Stretching out of this hole is a large granite spire stabbing deep into
the ground like an ancient sword piercing the earth. Its base is cracked and
crumbling while the spire itself remains smooth, narrowing in width as it enters
the ground. Looking skyward one can see this was once the crowning jewel of the
tower that stands at the centre of these ruins. From the blunt nature of the
cracks in the granite, it is apparent that this decapitation was not the result
of the towers age but rather caused by a deliberate act of aggression.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
The shimmering copper gates that once both welcomed and forbade entry into this
ancient city have turned green, corroded over time. The carved hinges from which
they once must have swung open with ease now barely support the small piece of
the gate that still hangs upon them. The rest of the gate lies on the ground in
splintered fragments as if knocked down by a great force. Beginning here and
carrying on westwards is a cobblestone street possibly leading to the barely
visible remains of the city.
name: Remnants of the City Gate

desc:
Several wide granite steps lead from this hall down into the water filled
channel to the west. Looking closer however, one can see that unlike the steps
on the western side of the channel these are merely decorative, stopping just
under the water level. The water rushes past this point with great intensity,
yet very little of it ever escapes the conduit. The light from small blue flames
in tiny lanterns lining the eastern wall casts a cool glow on the granite floor.
name: Coruscate Hall Steps

desc:
This wide hall is bordered by a granite wall to the east and a water filled
channel to the west. The air is humid and the rushing water creates a soft, cool
wind through this enormous open area. The hall here matches the one west of the
channel. The entire area shares the same curved ceiling that arches above the
channel. It must have taken years for teams of elves to carve this conduit that
diverts the water from a geyser north of here through this level of the
underground. Cut right through the east wall is a doorway surrounded by deeply
carved elven runes.
name: Coruscate Hall

desc:
Glass globes, in two rows, line the ceiling of this intricate workshop giving
off a soft white light. Along the back wall two furnaces used in the art of
glassblowing lie dormant. Detailing tools are arranged haphazardly about the
room amid the splintered remains of workbenches. Shards of multicoloured glass
lie strewn about the shop causing the floor to sparkle.
name: Glass Works

desc:
Beautiful silver chains shine above the mist as they support the swaying bridge
of metal slats across the fast flowing water. The rush of water creates a cool
wind blowing through this carved channel causing the metal bridge to clink from
time to time like the ringing of tiny bells. Each section of chain is in
actuality made up of six finely crafted silver chains around an iron support
rod. The flat wide slats that make up the floor of the bridge are heavy iron ore
with fine silver folded over them several times to feign the appearance of solid
silver yet with the strength to support the elves as they crossed. A bridge of
stone would surely have been sturdier but clearly the elves went to great
lengths to practice their craft in constructing this work of art with function.
name: Gilded Suspension Bridge

desc:
Water flows through a channel just east of this wide platform. To the west is a
large arched doorway cut into the granite wall. All down the sides and across
the arch of the doorway are deeply carved elven runes. Small cast iron lamps,
lining the space between the wall and ceiling, give off a soft light. Amazingly,
their tiny blue flames show no source of being fed by oil or wood. Less than
five of the lamps that line both of these great halls along the channel do not
burn; they now are home to a few small spider webs. The ceiling curves upward to
an arch above the channelled water and looks to curve back downward above a
parallel ledge to this one far across the bridge to the east.
name: Candescence Hall

desc:
The dominating structure in this hollowed out workshop is a massive forge
embedded into the western wall. Now dormant, this forge seems to have once been
used to heat metals of varying sizes, before being hammered into shape on black
anvils, arranged in a row down the centre of the shop. Looking upward, small air
vents can been seen drawing air out from the chamber, providing a slight breeze.
A fine layer of charcoal coloured soot covers the walls and floor.
name: Metal Works

desc:
The small iron lanterns along the west wall provide light enough to see the
cascade of granite steps that lead into the channel of water to the east. The
tiny blue flames illuminate the water as it rushes over all but the top two
steps. Looking over the edge of this platform it is clear to see the steps
continue down, narrowing as they reach the bottom of the channel. Across the
channel one can see identical steps leading down to the same underwater spot.
Surely these stairs are not simply decorative and the water rushes too quickly
for swimming. A long metal chain with a looping harness extends from the base of
the first step.
name: Candescence Hall Steps

desc:
This round concourse connecting the long halls at its north and south ends seems
to have little other purpose than to create a more open environment than is
common to places deep underground. A painted domed ceiling contributes to the
illusion. Looking up one can see a pale blue sky with soft white clouds, so
beautifully detailed only the absence of sunlight reveals the deception.
Carvings in the walls resemble large mellyrn with pure gold leaf forming their
delicate leaves. The moulding where the walls meet the floor has been shaped
into small flowers, their petals kissed with shimmering silver leaf. Several
park benches made of the palest granite line the walls. The sound of running
water can be heard, accompanied by a cool draft emanating from the south.
name: Stately Concourse

desc:
Two granite columns, smaller versions of the ones that line the hall above, mark
the entrance to this lower level from the stairway to the north. The columns
span the height of this corridor from the worn granite tile floor to the
slightly arched ceiling. The stone walls, which one would expect to be cold this
far underground, instead seems to carry a resonating warmth. Runes carved deep
into the walls seem to have been burned into the stone rather than merely
carved. Each letter is replete with black scoring making the letters stand out
in colour from the wall itself. An arched doorway has been carved out of the
solid granite of the western wall.
name: Along a Resplendent Corridor

desc:
Carved rectangular holes in the granite walls form compartments set into every
wall in this room. Silver gates hang on hinges as doors to some of the larger
sections. Other compartments have solid metal doors several inches thick. The
doors however, were not strong enough to protect the safes' valuable contents
from whomever ransacked this room. The floor is covered in broken bits of finely
crafted objects made from silver, gold, and glass. Inside one of these gated
cabinets is a thin metal blade, the metal has been folded over heat several
times but it is nowhere near the thickness needed to do any damage. Clearly a
useless unfinished work long ago abandoned.
name: Regal Repository

desc:
This small space is dominated by a long cylindrical stairway leading upward step
by step in a spiral design. Made of iron, this well constructed stairway is
firmly planted in the granite flooring, its only point of attachment at this
level. The north wall behind the stairs has been carved at the same rate of
curvature and polished smooth. The wall and the stair coexist without ever
touching and are just far enough apart that an outstretched arm from the stair
could not touch the wall.
name: Cylindrical Stairwell

desc:
A stream of water several feet wide flows quickly down from an opening in the
granite bluff just a short distance above. The cascade of water arches through
the air about arms length from the side of the bluff to the north before
crashing into the river at this point with tremendous force.
name: Infusion of the Water

desc:
There is no air above the fast flowing water. Stretching out one's limbs, both
the top and bottom of this chute can be touched. There is nothing, however, to
grab hold of as...
name: Deluged Chute

desc:
The crystal clear water pushes southward without hesitation. About fifteen feet
deep at its centre this conduit appears to redirect an underground geyser to a
venting point close by. The granite channel walls are nicked in many places by
what looks to have been metal weapons or perhaps even hooks, giving evidence
that something or someone knew better than to let the current carry them any
further.
name: Fluvial Aqueduct

desc:
The suspended silvery bridge above hangs far above the water's crest out of
reach of even the most proficient of swimmers. Those trying to keep their head
above water are quickly sucked under, barely being able to take even a single
breath. Just north of here the water flows straight upward with tremendous
force, crashing back down upon itself, and then shoots southward. The water
here, unlike that to the south, is almost impossible to see through due to the
bi-directional current.
name: Fluvial Aqueduct

desc:
The source of this surging aqueduct lies below, an enormous venting of water
through a large crack in the granite bluff, hurling everything in its path into
the geyser above.
name: Intrepid Primordial Vent

desc:
Under the phosphorescent foamy crest of the waters rushing through this
elven-made conduit the water is crystal clear. Holding one's breath while
floating in about fifteen feet and looking downward through the rushing water
one can see a solid metal door made of an unusual alloy; a mix of silver, iron
and other metals. The current is very strong.
name: Fluvial Aqueduct

desc:
Encased in a chamber with four smooth granite walls, a squat ceiling and a
grooved metallic floor, there is little to attract attention save for the
reinforced metal door, with no apparent knob or handle, providing exit to the
west. If examined closely tiny holes can be seen in the flooring, perhaps
allowing water escaping in from the sliding metal panel above to flow out down
below.
name: Siphoning Chamber

desc:
Two slender polished handrails, reflecting the soft blue glow of lanterns
running the length of this dimly lit corridor, assist travellers as they labour
the sixty-three steps between two carved granite archways. This long passageway
descends gradually from east to west. The smooth granite steps contain a
continual etched pattern along the area where they meet the smooth stone walls.
Other than these few decorative accents the stairway is rather simplistic.
name: Burnished Banister

desc:
Strikingly, this chamber appears to have been untouched by the passage of time.
Carved out of the granite and polished smooth the walls, floor, and ceiling are
perfectly symmetrical. Skylight pours into the centre of the room through a
large duct appearing to rise all the way up to the surface. Two tall archways,
one leading north the other east, exit the room.
name: Pool of Light

desc:
Carved out of the granite rock this room is quite extraordinary. Where most
rooms are filled with furniture and tools here the benches, the tool shelves and
the forge itself are all part of the room, as if the creators sculpted out of
the rock the area in which they would work and move. From the recess of the
circular forge emanates a cool blue flame. It flickers just slightly, keeping an
elegant shape, its tip white hot and its length reflecting its enchanted blue
light on the walls.
name: The Enchanted Forge

desc:
After twisting about a bit, the cave comes to an abrupt end in a small sized
chamber. A stone shelf has been cut into one side of the cave, which looks as if
it hasn't been used in years. Water seeps through the walls of this room and it
runs towards a large hole in the centre of the floor. The water makes the floor
of the cave very slippery.
name: Watery Chamber

desc:
Widening to the west, this cavern twists to the south. Many rocks protrude from
the walls of the passage and in places it is difficult to squeeze between them.
It looks like there may be a large chamber at the western end of the passage,
but it is difficult to tell.
name: Twisty Cave Tunnel

desc:
The rather odd set of stalagmites in the centre of the cavern are dwarfed by the
sheer size of this immense cavern. The stalagmites seem to be leaning towards
the northern edge of the cavern,while the ceiling above has a similarly strange
set of stalagtites hanging from various rocky protrusions. This room is guarded
from the spirits that roam the rest of the complex by a cleverly crafted secret
door hidden in one of the stalagmites.
name: Bed Cavern

desc:
Shadows dance around the rough walls of this passage which must have been cut by
a huge volume of water at some time in the past. However, the rocky sides appear
to have been chipped away in places to allow something easier passage. The
chippings now cover the rough floor of the cave, making it virtually flat.
name: Shadowy Cave

desc:
Stalactites hang from the ceiling of this cavern, which in times gone by was
carved from the rock by a large flow of water. The ground is very smooth and
slippery, and faint traces of algae and moss indicate that it is usually damp.
The walls are not so smooth, but natural passages branch out to the north and
west.
name: Small Cavern

desc:
The ceiling of this huge natural cavern is covered with stalactites high above.
The air feels cool and damp, but no breeze can be detected. Faint traces of moss
and lichen grow on the rocks about you. The frequent passing of some kind of
creature seems to have worn a smooth path in the rocky cavern floor.
name: Large Cavern

desc:
Rocky protrusions hinder your movement along this twisting natural passage,
which appears to have been carved by a great flow of water at one time.
Presumably the water has found another path now. The channel twists from east to
north here.
name: Narrow Cave

desc:
While the sections of wrought iron fence are mangled almost beyond recognition
the posts of the fencing still stand. Through the years saplings that most
assuredly would have been cut down to preserve the lawn have grown into trees
that now tower several stories high. The fencing is nothing more than a busy
tangle at the foot of their trunks. In the shadow of one of the larger trees is
a gap in the mess of sharp splinters of iron. A gate hangs closed by its last
remaining fasteners. It seems to be frozen in time vulnerable to fall at the
mercy of a strong wind. Perhaps it has survived only because of the trees that
shelter it from the winds and hard rains.
name: At the Old Iron Gate

desc:
The water travels quickly down a sharp slope in the cavern here, travelling in a
narrow ditch near the north of the cave. The cavern is smooth, hewn out by
centuries of the water's passage. To the east, some faint light can be seen.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
Gently undulating plains run from here down to the rivers edge to the
north-east. The occasional bush of holly breaks the rolling grasses. To the
south-west the holly bushes get thicker and possibly impassable in places.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Water exits here through a hole carved out of the southern face of the granite
bluff. Above is the old elven city of Ost-in-Edhil. Below a confluence of rivers
and rocky outcroppings. A vertical stream of white frothy water carries
everything flowing from the channel inside the bluff straight down to the waters
below.
name: Surging Waterfall

desc:
In this Noldorin chamber, steeped in ancient craftsmanship, subtle echoes of
Eregion's past intertwine with elven artistry. The focal point is a small forge
emitting a soft azure glow, surrounded by finely detailed Elvish runes on simple
stone walls. On the floor, a discreet circle gently shimmers with energy,
leaving a faint trail and carrying remnants of Eregion's bygone splendour. This
room stands as a testament to the enduring artistry of the Noldorin people,
seamlessly blending timeless craftsmanship with the hushed echoes of Eregion's
faded history.
name: The Noldorin Forge
